title: Thrown stoneware lidded jar
subtitle: A wheel-thrown jar and matching lid, sized with calipers, bisque-fired and glaze-fired at cone 6
excerpt: A lidded jar is two thrown forms made to fit: the jar body with an inside gallery to receive the lid, and a separate lid thrown to sit cleanly on that gallery. The match is set with calipers at the leather-hard stage, then both pieces dry, bisque-fire, glaze and glaze-fire together. This tutorial covers a 1.2 kg storage jar around 140 mm tall in smooth stoneware, fired at cone 6.
sourceNotes: Lidded jar construction derived from first principles. V&A open-access collection for the historical-form reference (English slipware and stoneware lidded storage jars). Cassell's The Book of the Home (1880s editions). Orton Ceramic Foundation cone temperature reference.

glossaryTerms.definition: Pushing the clay mound into a state where it rotates without wobbling under the hands.
The drying stage where clay holds its shape but is still damp enough to trim and join.
Air-dried but unfired clay. The final stage before bisque firing.
The first kiln firing, around cone 06 (999°C), that converts greenware into porous bisqueware ready for glazing.
A ledge thrown into the inside rim of a jar to receive a lid. The lid sits flush on the gallery.
A recessed ring trimmed into the base that lifts the piece off the kiln shelf and refines the base profile.
Water-based wax brushed onto bisque to mask areas the glaze should not touch.

projectSchedule.title: Throw body and lid
projectSchedule.body: Centre 900 g for the jar body; throw a cylinder, swell the belly, set an inside gallery. Then throw the lid from 300 g, measuring with calipers to match the gallery.

projectSchedule.title: Check fit at leather-hard
projectSchedule.body: Place the lid on the body and rotate gently. If too loose, the lid was thrown too small. If too tight, the lid binds on shrinkage. Trim to fit at leather-hard.

projectSchedule.title: Trim foot rings
projectSchedule.body: Trim a foot ring on the jar body and on the underside of the lid knob.

projectSchedule.title: Bone-dry check before bisque
projectSchedule.body: Both pieces must reach uniform room temperature throughout. The lid usually dries faster than the body.

projectSchedule.title: Bisque fire at cone 06
projectSchedule.body: Load the body and lid together; the lid can sit on the body during bisque firing because there is no glaze. Fire to cone 06 (999°C). Cool inside the kiln.

projectSchedule.title: Wax gallery rim and lid edge, then glaze
projectSchedule.body: Apply wax resist to the gallery surface and the matching lid edge so the lid will not fuse to the body. Dip the body and lid separately. Touch up.

projectSchedule.title: Glaze fire at cone 6
projectSchedule.body: Fire the body and lid separately in the kiln; do not stack one on the other in glaze firing. Cool below 80°C before opening.

content.text: A lidded jar is the test piece for measured throwing. The body and the lid are made separately; they must fit each other after shrinking by 12-13% in the firings. Match the diameters at the wet stage with calipers and the fit holds through to the finished pot. Get the calipers wrong and the lid is loose or jams permanently. This tutorial works in 1.2 kg of smooth stoneware total (900 g for the body, 300 g for the lid) producing a storage jar around 140 mm tall and 110 mm across, holding roughly 700 ml. A potter's wheel and access to a kiln firing to cone 6 are both required.

attrs.title: Silica + kiln safety
attrs.body: Silica dust is the chronic studio hazard and silicosis is irreversible. Mix wet whenever possible. Wear a P100 respirator any time raw glaze powders are open. Wet-clean only. Greenware sanding happens outdoors with a mask and never in a kitchen-air-shared space. For the kiln firing: ensure ventilation by downdraft vent or by an open window with an extractor pulling air past the kiln. Wear welder's shade-5 glasses when peephole-watching at high temperatures. Never open a hot kiln below 80°C. Keep a Class A fire extinguisher within reach of the studio area.

items.name: Pottery calipers
items.substitution: sprung-arm calipers for inside-to-outside diameter transfer

content.text: Throwing the body
1. Centre 900 g and throw a cylinder
Wedge 900 g and slap onto a bat attached to the wheel.
Centring

is complete when the mound feels still under the palms. Open to a floor 100 mm wide leaving a 6 mm floor thickness. Pull a cylinder in three passes to about 130 mm tall with walls 6 mm thick. The top 25 mm of wall will become the gallery zone, so leave the upper wall slightly thicker (about 8 mm) than the body wall below it.
2. Swell the belly slightly

Slow the wheel. Press the inside hand gently outward at the lower third of the cylinder, supporting the outside wall with a flat hand on the outside. Swell the body outward by 8-10 mm to a maximum diameter of about 110 mm. The shoulder above the belly tapers gently inward toward the gallery.
3. Throw the gallery
The
gallery

is an inside ledge that receives the lid. To throw it, split the rim into two thicknesses: an inner ledge and a thinner outer wall. Press the pad of the inside index finger downward into the top of the wall, about 4 mm in from the outer edge. The finger creates a step: the outer rim stays at its original height (the lid sits inside this); the inner ledge sits 6-8 mm lower (the lid rests on this surface).

The gallery surface needs to be horizontal (not sloping inward or outward) for the lid to sit flat. Smooth and level it with the side of the index finger or a small angled rib. The outer rim of the body is now a thin upstand 4 mm wide that contains and centres the lid.
4. Measure the gallery diameter with calipers

Stop the wheel. Open the calipers to the inside diameter of the gallery; the distance across the gallery ledge from one inner rim edge to the other. This measurement might be 90 mm. Note it. The lid will need to be thrown to fit this diameter exactly at the wet stage, knowing that body and lid will shrink at the same percentage in firing and will continue to match.
Wire off the body, lift the bat off, and set aside. Cover with light plastic so the gallery does not stiffen before the lid is thrown; both pieces need to reach leather-hard together.
Throwing the lid

Centre 300 g of stoneware on a fresh bat. Flatten the centred mound to about 25 mm thick and 90 mm wide; wider than the final lid because the edge will be trimmed back. Open the centre slightly with the thumbs to a 30 mm wide depression, leaving a low knob in the middle. Pull the edge of the disc outward and slightly upward to form a low flange that will sit over the gallery.

Stop the wheel. Hold the calipers (still set to the gallery diameter) against the outside edge of the lid flange. Trim the flange diameter with the needle tool until the calipers close cleanly on it. The lid is now sized to match the body.

Shape the centre knob upward into a small lifting handle; about 25 mm tall, 30 mm wide at the base, tapering toward a rounded top. Compress the knob with damp fingers. Wire off the lid and set aside, covered with plastic.
Checking the fit at leather-hard
After 8-24 hours both body and lid reach
leather-hard

. Lift the lid carefully and set it onto the body gallery. Rotate gently. A working fit drops the lid in cleanly with about 1 mm of play around the edge; enough to lift the lid out without binding but tight enough that it does not rattle. If the lid is too tight, scrape a small amount of clay off the outer edge of the lid flange with a fettling knife. If too loose, the lid was thrown too small; for the next jar, set the calipers slightly outside the gallery measurement to allow for the working clearance.
Trimming the foot rings
Invert the body on a foam pad or clay collar centred on the wheel head. Trim a
foot ring
90 mm in diameter and 4 mm proud at the base of the jar. Bevel both edges.

Invert the lid on the wheel head (the knob now points downward into a clay support cup or foam pad). Trim a small flat foot circle around the knob base on the underside of the lid (about 20 mm diameter and 2 mm proud) so the lid, when inverted on the bench, sits flat rather than rolling on the knob.
Drying and bisque firing
Place the lid on the body and dry both together; this keeps the fit accurate as the shrinkage progresses. Cover loosely with plastic for the first 48 hours, then uncover and dry to
bone-dry
over four to seven days. Load both pieces together for the
bisque fire
; the lid can sit on the body in the bisque firing because there is no glaze to fuse them. Fire to cone 06 (999°C). Cool inside the kiln to room temperature.
Glazing
Wipe both pieces with a damp sponge. Apply
wax resist

to the foot ring of the body and to the entire gallery surface that the lid sits on. Apply wax resist to the matching edge of the lid; the flange surface that contacts the gallery. Without the wax, the molten glaze fuses the lid to the body in the firing and the jar becomes a permanently sealed object.

Pour glaze inside the body first (about 100-150 ml) rotate to coat the interior, and tip out the excess. Then dip the body exterior into the glaze bucket, holding by the foot ring, submerging fully, rotating as it lifts clear. Touch up finger marks.
Dip the lid separately, holding by the knob. Submerge fully, rotate, lift. Touch up the knob mark. Both pieces dry separately on a rack.
Glaze firing

Load the body and lid on the kiln shelf separately. Do not stack the lid on the body in the glaze firing even though they were waxed; glaze surface tension at full melt is unpredictable and any flaw in the wax line will fuse the pieces. Fire to cone 6 (1222°C). Cool with the lid closed until the kiln reads 80°C or below before opening.
What it looks like when it is right

The fired lid drops onto the gallery cleanly with about 1 mm of working play. It rotates freely without sticking but does not rattle in place. The glaze surface is even on both pieces; the wax-resist line on the gallery and the matching lid edge fires to bare stoneware; slightly darker than the glazed surface, which is the correct look for a functional jar. Lifted off and replaced, the lid lands true every time without binding or wobbling.
Variations

A smaller storage jar at 600 g body + 200 g lid follows the same sequence at smaller scale and holds around 350 ml. A taller jar with a domed lid uses 1.5 kg body + 400 g lid and gives more interior space; the dome is thrown by leaving more clay above the gallery and shaping it into a soft curve rather than flattening to a disc.

items.symptom: Lid binds in the gallery after firing (will not lift off)
items.cause: Lid was waxed but glaze drip bridged the wax line, or the lid was thrown without working clearance
items.fix: Set calipers 1-2 mm wider than the gallery to leave room. Brush wax in two coats; the second coat at right angles to the first. Check the wax cover by eye before glazing

items.symptom: Lid rattles loose in the gallery after firing
items.cause: The caliper reading took in the gallery upstand thickness. That made the lid too small for the real seating width
items.fix: Measure the gallery seating face, not the outer rim. Set calipers to the inside of the upstand, not the outside

items.symptom: Lid warps during drying or firing
items.cause: Lid was too thin in the centre, or dried unevenly
items.fix: Throw the lid with a 5-6 mm thickness across the disc; dry the lid on the body to ensure even shrinkage on both pieces

items.symptom: Crack appears at the inside corner of the gallery
items.cause: Compression weak at the corner where the inside ledge meets the wall
items.fix: Press the corner with a small wooden tool while throwing. The inside corner takes the most stress and needs firm pressing

items.symptom: Body and lid have different glaze colours after firing
items.cause: One piece sat in a hotter spot in the kiln, or glaze on one was thicker
items.fix: Load both on the same shelf level. Dip both for the same count (two seconds is the norm). Move the kiln spot on later firings to spot hot zones
